sections.title: What is a Budget?
sections.content: A budget is a quantitative expression of a plan for a specific future period. It includes planned revenues, costs, cash flows, and capital expenditures. Budgeting translates strategic goals into operational targets and provides a roadmap for managers to follow. It is a critical tool for planning, coordination, motivation, and control.

sections.title: Functions of Budgeting
sections.content:
**1. Planning** вЂ” Forces managers to think ahead, set goals, and identify resource needs before the period begins.

**2. Communication & Coordination** вЂ” Aligns all departments toward common targets, ensuring departments work toward organisational objectives.

**3. Motivation** вЂ” Gives teams specific, measurable goals to work toward, encouraging effort and performance.

**4. Control** вЂ” Provides benchmarks to compare actual performance against planned, enabling management by exception (focus on variances).

These four functions make budgeting essential for effective organisational management.

sections.title: Budgeting Approaches
sections.content:
**Top-Down Budgeting:**
- Senior management sets revenue and spending targets for each department
- Advantage: Fast, ensures alignment with overall strategy
- Disadvantage: May ignore operational realities, departments feel pressure

**Participative (Bottom-Up) Budgeting:**
- Department managers provide input; senior management reviews and consolidates
- Advantage: More accurate (based on real operational knowledge), higher buy-in
- Disadvantage: Time-consuming, risk of budget padding (managers padding to create slack)

**Best Practice:** Combination of both вЂ” top-down guidance with participative input.

sections.title: Budgeting Strategies
sections.content:
**Incremental Budgeting:**
- Starts with prior period figures and adjusts up/down
- Advantage: Simple, quick, understands historical baseline
- Disadvantage: Entrenches past inefficiencies, doesn't question old spending

**Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB):**
- Starts from zero; every expenditure must be justified from scratch
- Advantage: Eliminates waste, reallocates resources optimally, challenges assumptions
- Disadvantage: Extremely time-consuming, may demotivate managers

**Rolling Budget:**
- Continuously updated; adds a new period as prior period expires
- Keeps budget current and forward-looking

**Periodic Budget:**
- Fixed annual or quarterly period, not updated mid-period

sections.title: Identify the Budgeting Approach
sections.content: Test your understanding of budgeting methods.

question.prompt: A manager is required to justify every single line item of their budget from zero, regardless of what was spent last year. This is:
question.explanation: Zero-based budgeting requires all expenditures to be justified from scratch each period. It eliminates the assumption that last year's spending was necessary or appropriate.

sections.title: The Master Budget
sections.content:
The master budget is an integrated set of budgets for the whole organisation.

**Typical Sequence:**
1. Sales Budget (starting point)
2. Production Budget
3. Direct Materials Budget (purchases and costs)
4. Direct Labour Budget
5. Manufacturing Overhead Budget
6. Cost of Goods Sold Budget
7. Selling & Admin Expense Budget
8. Cash Budget (integrates all cash flows)
9. Budgeted Income Statement (profit forecast)
10. Budgeted Balance Sheet (financial position forecast)

Each budget builds on the prior one. All are interconnected and must be internally consistent.

sections.title: Budget Preparation: Sales to Production
sections.content:
**Sales Budget:**
- Expected unit sales: 1,000 units
- Selling price: $50
- Budgeted revenue: 1,000 Г— $50 = $50,000

**Production Budget:**
- Expected sales: 1,000 units
- Add: Desired ending inventory: 200 units
- Less: Beginning inventory: 100 units
- Units to produce: 1,000 + 200 - 100 = 1,100 units

**Direct Materials Budget:**
- Units to produce: 1,100
- Materials per unit: 2 kg
- Materials needed: 1,100 Г— 2 = 2,200 kg
- Add: Desired ending inventory: 300 kg
- Less: Beginning inventory: 200 kg
- Materials to purchase: 2,200 + 300 - 200 = 2,300 kg

sections.title: Cash Budget
sections.content:
The cash budget forecasts cash inflows and outflows to prevent cash shortfalls.

**Cash Budget = Beginning Cash + Inflows - Outflows = Ending Cash**

Includes:
- Collections from customers (may lag sales)
- Payments for materials (may lag purchases)
- Payments for labour and overhead
- Interest and debt payments
- Dividends, capital expenditures

The cash budget identifies periods of surplus (opportunities for investment) or shortfall (need for borrowing). Critical for managing working capital and ensuring liquidity.

sections.title: Identify the Budget Sequence
sections.content: Test your knowledge of master budget preparation order.

question.prompt: Which budget must be prepared FIRST before all others in the master budget?
question.explanation: The Sales Budget is always first. All other budgets (production, materials, labour, overhead, cash) depend on projected sales volume. Without sales, you can't plan production or any other function.

sections.title: Static vs Flexible Budget в­ђ Exam Topic
sections.content:
This framework is tested in **every SUSS ACC202 exam**. You must know all three columns and the two variances.

---

**Static Budget** = The original budget prepared at the START of the period, based on *planned* sales volume.

**Flexible Budget** = The budget REBASED to *actual* sales volume, but keeping original unit costs/prices.

**Actual Results** = What really happened.

---

**The Three-Column Framework:**

| | Static Budget | Flexible Budget | Actual Results |
|---|---|---|---|
| Units | Planned volume | **Actual volume** | Actual volume |
| Revenue | Planned price Г— Planned units | Planned price Г— **Actual units** | Actual price Г— Actual units |
| Variable Costs | Planned VC/unit Г— Planned units | Planned VC/unit Г— **Actual units** | Actual VC/unit Г— Actual units |
| Fixed Costs | Budgeted fixed | Budgeted fixed | Actual fixed |
| **Operating Profit** | X | Y | Z |

---

**Two Key Variances:**

**Sales Volume Variance (SVV)** = Flexible Budget Profit в€’ Static Budget Profit
- Caused by selling MORE or FEWER units than planned
- F if actual volume > budgeted volume
- U if actual volume < budgeted volume

**Flexible Budget Variance (FBV)** = Actual Results в€’ Flexible Budget
- Caused by PRICES or COSTS being different from standard
- F if actual profit > flexible budget profit
- U if actual profit < flexible budget profit

**Total Variance** = Actual Profit в€’ Static Budget Profit = SVV + FBV

---

**Memory tip:** Think of it as two questions:
1. *Did we sell the right volume?* в†’ Sales Volume Variance
2. *Did we charge/spend the right amounts?* в†’ Flexible Budget Variance

sections.title: Static vs Flexible Budget: Worked Example в­ђ
sections.content:
**Given:**
- Budgeted sales: 1,000 units at $50 each
- Variable cost: $30 per unit
- Fixed costs: $8,000
- Actual sales: 1,200 units at $48 each
- Actual variable cost: $31 per unit
- Actual fixed costs: $8,500

---

**Step 1 вЂ” Static Budget (at planned 1,000 units):**
- Revenue: 1,000 Г— $50 = $50,000
- Variable Costs: 1,000 Г— $30 = $30,000
- Fixed Costs: $8,000
- **Static Budget Profit = $12,000**

**Step 2 вЂ” Flexible Budget (at actual 1,200 units, standard prices):**
- Revenue: 1,200 Г— $50 = $60,000
- Variable Costs: 1,200 Г— $30 = $36,000
- Fixed Costs: $8,000 (unchanged)
- **Flexible Budget Profit = $16,000**

**Step 3 вЂ” Actual Results:**
- Revenue: 1,200 Г— $48 = $57,600
- Variable Costs: 1,200 Г— $31 = $37,200
- Fixed Costs: $8,500
- **Actual Profit = $11,900**

---

**Variance Calculations:**

**Sales Volume Variance** = Flexible в€’ Static = $16,000 в€’ $12,000 = **$4,000 F**
в†’ Sold 200 more units than planned (good)

**Flexible Budget Variance** = Actual в€’ Flexible = $11,900 в€’ $16,000 = **$4,100 U**
в†’ Lower selling price ($48 vs $50) and higher costs dragged profit down

**Total Variance** = Actual в€’ Static = $11,900 в€’ $12,000 = **$100 U**
= $4,000 F + $4,100 U = $100 U вњ“

**Interpretation:** We sold more units (good вЂ” $4,000 F volume variance) but our prices were lower and costs were higher than expected (bad вЂ” $4,100 U), resulting in a small overall unfavourable variance.

question.prompt: Static Budget Profit = $20,000. Flexible Budget Profit (at actual volume) = $25,000. Actual Profit = $22,000. What is the Sales Volume Variance and is it F or U?
choices: $3,000 U вЂ” actual profit is less than flexible budget
$5,000 F вЂ” flexible budget profit exceeds static budget profit
$2,000 U вЂ” actual profit is less than static budget
$5,000 U вЂ” flexible budget exceeds actual results
question.explanation: Sales Volume Variance = Flexible Budget Profit в€’ Static Budget Profit = $25,000 в€’ $20,000 = $5,000 F. This is FAVOURABLE because the company sold more units than planned, generating more profit at standard prices. The Flexible Budget Variance = Actual в€’ Flexible = $22,000 в€’ $25,000 = $3,000 U (prices/costs were worse than standard).

sections.title: Variance Analysis вЂ” Revenue and Cost Detail
sections.content:
Comparing actual results against the budget identifies variances.

**Favourable (F) Variance:** Actual profit > Budgeted profit (good)
**Unfavourable (U) Variance:** Actual profit < Budgeted profit (concerning)

**Revenue Variance** = Actual Revenue в€’ Budgeted Revenue
- F if actual > budget (more revenue earned)

**Cost Variance** = Budgeted Cost в€’ Actual Cost
- F if actual costs are LOWER (you spent less than planned)
- U if actual costs are HIGHER (you overspent)

Note: For costs, the sign convention is REVERSED from revenue вЂ” spending less is favourable.

Variance analysis supports **management by exception** вЂ” managers focus attention on significant deviations that require explanation and corrective action. Small variances are normal and expected.

sections.title: Budgetary Control Cycle
sections.content:
1. **Set Budget** вЂ” Develop and approve budget targets
2. **Measure Actual Performance** вЂ” Track actual results during the period
3. **Compare Actual vs Budget** вЂ” Calculate variances
4. **Analyse Variances** вЂ” Investigate significant deviations and identify root causes
5. **Take Corrective Action** вЂ” Adjust operations or update expectations
6. **Revise Budget if Necessary** вЂ” Update budget if circumstances change significantly

This continuous cycle is central to management control and ensures the organisation remains on track toward objectives.

questions.prompt: Explain the difference between incremental and zero-based budgeting, and identify one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
questions.modelAnswer: Incremental budgeting adjusts prior period figures up or down. Advantage: quick and simple. Disadvantage: may perpetuate past inefficiencies and unnecessary spending. ZBB starts from zero requiring full justification of every line item. Advantage: eliminates waste and reallocates resources optimally. Disadvantage: extremely time-consuming and may demotivate managers. Choice depends on organisational culture and resources available.
keyPoints: Incremental = prior period + adjustments
ZBB = justify every line from zero
Incremental is faster, ZBB is more thorough
Each has different cost-benefit trade-offs

questions.prompt: What is the master budget and why must the sales budget be prepared first?
questions.modelAnswer: The master budget is an integrated set of functional budgets covering the entire organisation (sales, production, materials, labour, overhead, cash, and financial statements). The sales budget must come first because projected sales volume drives all other budgets. Production depends on how many units to sell; materials and labour depend on production quantity; overhead allocation depends on production level; cash budget depends on cash flows from all these activities. Without sales projections, no other budget can be meaningfully prepared.
keyPoints: Master budget = integrated operational plans
Sales budget is the starting point
All other budgets depend on sales volume
Sequence ensures logical flow and consistency

questions.prompt: What is variance analysis and how does it support management decision-making?
questions.modelAnswer: Variance analysis compares actual results against the budget to identify and explain differences. Favourable variances (actual profit > budget) are positive; unfavourable variances signal problems requiring investigation. It supports management by exception вЂ” directing management attention to significant deviations that require explanation or corrective action. For example, an unfavourable revenue variance may trigger investigation of pricing, market conditions, or sales team performance. Variance analysis enables fact-based decision-making and accountability.
keyPoints: Variance = Actual vs Budget difference
Favourable (F) vs Unfavourable (U)
Management by exception = focus on significant deviations
Identifies root causes for corrective action

questions.prompt:
A company budgeted to sell 2,000 units at $60 each, variable cost $35 per unit, fixed costs $15,000. Actual results: 2,500 units sold at $58 each, actual variable cost $36 per unit, actual fixed costs $16,000.

(a) Prepare the Static Budget, Flexible Budget, and Actual Results in a three-column format, showing Revenue, Variable Costs, Fixed Costs, and Operating Profit.
(b) Calculate the Sales Volume Variance and state whether Favourable (F) or Unfavourable (U).
(c) Calculate the Flexible Budget Variance and state whether Favourable (F) or Unfavourable (U).
(d) Verify that Total Variance = Sales Volume Variance + Flexible Budget Variance.
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) Three-Column Analysis:

Static Budget (2,000 units):
- Revenue: 2,000 Г— $60 = $120,000
- Variable Costs: 2,000 Г— $35 = $70,000
- Fixed Costs: $15,000
- Operating Profit: $35,000

Flexible Budget (2,500 units, standard prices):
- Revenue: 2,500 Г— $60 = $150,000
- Variable Costs: 2,500 Г— $35 = $87,500
- Fixed Costs: $15,000
- Operating Profit: $47,500

Actual Results (2,500 units):
- Revenue: 2,500 Г— $58 = $145,000
- Variable Costs: 2,500 Г— $36 = $90,000
- Fixed Costs: $16,000
- Operating Profit: $39,000

(b) Sales Volume Variance = Flexible Budget Profit в€’ Static Budget Profit = $47,500 в€’ $35,000 = $12,500 F
Favourable because 500 more units were sold than planned.

(c) Flexible Budget Variance = Actual Profit в€’ Flexible Budget Profit = $39,000 в€’ $47,500 = $8,500 U
Unfavourable because selling price was $2 lower, variable cost $1 higher, and fixed costs $1,000 over budget.

(d) Total Variance = Actual в€’ Static = $39,000 в€’ $35,000 = $4,000 F
Verification: $12,500 F + $8,500 U = $4,000 F вњ“
keyPoints: Static Budget uses PLANNED volume and planned prices/costs
Flexible Budget uses ACTUAL volume but PLANNED (standard) prices/costs
Sales Volume Variance = Flexible Budget Profit в€’ Static Budget Profit
Flexible Budget Variance = Actual Profit в€’ Flexible Budget Profit
Total Variance = SVV + FBV (always verify this adds up)
F = Actual Profit > Budget; U = Actual Profit < Budget

questions.prompt:
в­ђ EXAM-STYLE QUESTION вЂ” Cash Budget (18 marks)

Apparel Ltd is preparing a cash budget for the first quarter of 20X6 (January, February, March). The following information is available:

1. Opening cash balance on 1 January 20X6: $18,000

2. Budgeted total monthly sales:
   January: $60,000 | February: $72,000 | March: $84,000
   вЂ” 25% of all sales are cash sales; 75% are credit sales
   вЂ” Credit sales: 60% collected in the month of sale, 40% collected the following month
   вЂ” December 20X5 credit sales were $52,500

3. Cost of Goods Sold: 40% of total sales, paid in the same month as sale

4. Monthly operating expenses: $14,000 total
   вЂ” Cash expenses: $11,500 per month
   вЂ” Depreciation: $2,500 per month (non-cash)

5. Wages: $9,000 per month, paid in the month incurred

6. Rent: $18,000 per year, paid quarterly вЂ” first payment in January

7. Bank loan of $30,000 taken at start of January. Annual interest rate 8%.
   Interest paid quarterly at end of March. Principal repaid at end of March.

Required: Prepare a Cash Budget for Apparel Ltd for the first quarter of 20X6, showing figures for each month (Jan, Feb, Mar) and a quarterly total.
questions.modelAnswer:
STEP 1 вЂ” Work out sales breakdown first:

                Jan       Feb       Mar
Total Sales    $60,000   $72,000   $84,000
Cash Sales(25%) $15,000  $18,000   $21,000
Credit Sales(75%)$45,000 $54,000   $63,000

STEP 2 вЂ” Work out credit collections:
вЂў Dec credit sales $52,500 в†’ 40% collected in Jan = $21,000
вЂў Jan credit sales $45,000 в†’ 60% in Jan = $27,000; 40% in Feb = $18,000
вЂў Feb credit sales $54,000 в†’ 60% in Feb = $32,400; 40% in Mar = $21,600
вЂў Mar credit sales $63,000 в†’ 60% in Mar = $37,800; 40% in Apr (outside quarter)

STEP 3 вЂ” Work out COGS payments (40% of total sales, paid same month):
Jan: $24,000 | Feb: $28,800 | Mar: $33,600

STEP 4 вЂ” Quarterly rent = $18,000 Г· 4 = $4,500 (paid January only)

STEP 5 вЂ” Interest = $30,000 Г— 8% Г· 4 = $600 (paid March only)

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
CASH BUDGET вЂ” Apparel Ltd
First Quarter 20X6
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
                          Jan        Feb        Mar        Q1 Total
Opening Cash Balance    $18,000    $62,000    $81,100

ADD: Cash Receipts
  Cash Sales             15,000     18,000     21,000
  Credit (same month)    27,000     32,400     37,800
  Credit (prior month)   21,000     18,000     21,600
Total Cash Available    $81,000   $130,400   $161,500

LESS: Cash Disbursements
  COGS (paid same month)  24,000     28,800     33,600
  Cash Operating Exp.     11,500     11,500     11,500
  Wages                    9,000      9,000      9,000
  Rent (quarterly)         4,500          -          -
Total Disbursements     $49,000    $49,300    $54,100

Excess/(Shortage)       $32,000    $81,100   $107,400

FINANCING
  Borrowing (loan)       +30,000          -          -
  Loan Repayment               -          -    (30,000)
  Interest Paid                -          -       (600)
Net Financing           $30,000         $0   ($30,600)

Closing Cash Balance    $62,000    $81,100    $76,800
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ

вљ пёЏ KEY RULES APPLIED:
вЂў Depreciation ($2,500/month) is EXCLUDED вЂ” it is not a cash item
вЂў Credit collections are split: 60% same month, 40% next month
вЂў December credit collections carried into January
вЂў Rent $18,000 Г· 4 = $4,500 per quarter, paid in January
вЂў Interest: $30,000 Г— 8% Г— (3/12) = $600, paid in March
вЂў Loan principal repaid in March: $30,000
keyPoints: в­ђ Depreciation is NEVER included in a cash budget вЂ” it is non-cash
Always split credit sales by collection timing (e.g., 60% this month, 40% next)
Prior month's closing balance = this month's opening balance
COGS if paid in the month of sale is a cash disbursement
Quarterly expenses: divide annual amount by 4, put in the right month
Interest = Principal Г— Rate Г— Time (convert to quarterly or monthly as needed)
Closing = Opening + Total Receipts в€’ Total Disbursements + Net Financing

questions.prompt:
в­ђ EXAM-STYLE QUESTION вЂ” Cash Status Evaluation (5 marks)

Using your cash budget for Apparel Ltd (Question su6_a5 above), evaluate the company's cash position for the first quarter of 20X6. Your answer should comment on:
(a) The overall cash trend during the quarter
(b) Whether the company faces any cash shortage
(c) Whether the bank loan was necessary
(d) Any recommendations for management
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) Overall cash trend:
Apparel Ltd's cash position strengthens significantly during the quarter. The closing balance grows from $18,000 (opening) to $62,000 (end of January), $81,100 (end of February), and $76,800 (end of March after loan repayment). The company generates positive cash flow in every month of the quarter, driven by growing sales volumes.

(b) Cash shortage:
The company does NOT face a cash shortage at any point during the quarter. Even before borrowing, the January excess was $32,000 вЂ” well above any reasonable minimum balance requirement. There is no month where a deficit occurs.

(c) Whether the bank loan was necessary:
The $30,000 bank loan taken in January appears UNNECESSARY based on the cash budget. The company already had $32,000 excess cash in January before any financing. The loan inflated the January and February closing balances but was repaid in March, costing $600 in interest. Management should assess whether the loan was precautionary (reasonable) or taken without analysing the cash budget (poor financial planning).

(d) Recommendations:
вЂў Cancel or defer future borrowing вЂ” cash flows are self-sustaining from operations
вЂў Invest surplus cash in short-term instruments to earn returns on the $62,000вЂ“$81,000 balances
вЂў Continue monitoring monthly to ensure growing sales (credit risk) are actually collected on time
вЂў Review whether 30-day credit terms (40% collection lag) can be tightened to improve cash inflows
keyPoints: Comment on trend (improving, declining, stable) not just a single number
Identify any months with shortage vs surplus clearly
Evaluate whether borrowing was justified given the cash position
Recommendations must be actionable and linked to the actual numbers
Good evaluation answers 'so what?' вЂ” not just describing numbers but interpreting them
